summary: Jeff Atwood recounts discovering that his wife preferred Google Maps over the desktop application Microsoft Streets and Trips he had championed for years. Testing revealed that the web app loaded three times faster and required dramatically fewer steps for common tasks like getting directions. He observes that web applications have become faster, simpler, and more feature-rich than their desktop counterparts, which remain stuck in outdated UI conventions. The post concludes that desktop mapping applications are effectively dead except for niche offline use cases.
key_points: Google Maps loads in 3 seconds versus 9 seconds for Streets and Trips on a fast desktop PC
Getting directions requires 8 steps in Streets and Trips but only 2 steps in Google Maps
Web applications have turned browser limitations into strengths by offering online-enabled features like satellite view and real-time traffic
Desktop applications remain stuck in circa-1999 UI conventions of toolbars, menus, and right-clicking while web apps evolve rapidly
Web apps offer continuous updates and are free or ad-subsidized, eliminating yearly upgrade cycles
money_quotes: It's a mixed-up, topsy-turvy world we live in when web mapping applications are now faster and more convenient to use than their desktop equivalents.
All the innovation in user interface seems to be taking place on the web, and desktop applications just aren't keeping up.

Web applications are evolving online at a frenetic pace, while most desktop applications are mired in circa-1999 desktop user interface conventions, plopping out yearly releases with barely noticeable new features.
Google Maps turns all those browser-based application weaknesses into strengths.
It's hard not to look at the facts and conclude that desktop applications – at least desktop mapping applications – are dead.
key_insight: The constraints of web applications have forced innovation in user experience that desktop applications, comfortable with their power advantage, have failed to pursue.
